summary:
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (August 26, 1789 AD) was the foundational document of the [[event:107|French Revolution]] and one of the most influential statements of political philosophy in human history, articulating universal principles of individual liberty, equality before the law, and popular sovereignty that would inspire revolutionary movements worldwide. Adopted by the National Constituent Assembly, the Declaration drew explicitly on [[event:2346|Enlightenment]] philosophy — particularly Rousseau's social contract, Locke's natural rights, and the [[event:2350|American Declaration of Independence]] (1776) — proclaiming that 'the principle of all sovereignty resides essentially in the nation' and that 'the aim of all political association is the preservation of the natural and imprescriptible rights of man,' defined as liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression.

The Declaration's seventeen articles established rights that remain central to liberal democratic theory: freedom of speech and press, freedom of religion, freedom from arbitrary arrest, presumption of innocence, equal access to public office based on merit, and taxation only with representative consent. Its universalist language ('Men are born and remain free and equal in rights') simultaneously inspired and contradicted the practice of the French Empire: the [[event:2435|enslaved people of Haiti]] used it to demand their own freedom in the [[event:2335|Haitian Revolution]], and Olympe de Gouges's Declaration of the Rights of Woman (1791) exposed the document's failure to include women. The Declaration became one of the foundational texts of human rights, embedded in successive French constitutions, referenced in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), and invoked in independence movements across two centuries.
